structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a single hospital system covering 36 months of admission data. Data includes all patients with complete records: vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, temperature) recorded at 4-hour intervals during hospitalization; medication administrations with timestamps; lab results with timestamps; and discharge summaries. Source: production EHR system with institutional data governance approval.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge (1 = readmitted, 0 = not readmitted). Readmission is defined as any unplanned hospital admission occurring within exactly 30 days of the initial discharge date, identified via admission timestamps in the EHR. Patients with planned readmissions (e.g., scheduled chemotherapy, elective surgery) are excluded.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split by admission date, with patient-level integrity preserved (all time-series data for a single patient stays in one split). Months 1–24 (first 24 months) for training; months 25–30 (months 7–12 of year 3) for validation; months 31–36 (final 6 months) for test set. Justification: (1) Respects the temporal structure of patient trajectories — models are trained on historical data and evaluated on prospective holdout periods, avoiding look-ahead bias. (2) Hospital practices evolve; temporal split captures drift in discharge procedures, staffing, and clinical guidelines. (3) Patient-level grouping prevents the same patient's data from appearing in multiple splits, which would create data leakage and overestimate model generalization. (4) 67% train / 17% validation / 17% test allocation is standard for time-series healthcare data with this volume.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformations (scaling, imputation, temporal feature engineering) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). The fitted transformers (scalers, imputers, encoders) are then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents information about the validation and test distributions from influencing the training process. Specifically: (1) Missing value imputation uses mean/median strategies computed from training data only; (2) Feature scaling (standardization) uses training set mean and standard deviation; (3) Temporal aggregations (e.g., rate of change in vital signs) are computed independently for each patient's time window but using training-set-derived statistics.
structured_choices.features: Time-series features engineered from raw signals: (1) Vital sign trajectories: mean, std dev, min, max, and linear trend slope for heart rate, BP, oxygen saturation, temperature computed over the full hospitalization window. (2) Medication features: count of distinct medications, count of high-risk medication classes (opioids, sedatives, vasopressors), duration on mechanical ventilation (if applicable). (3) Lab result features: abnormality flags (value outside reference range), trend direction, count of labs ordered. (4) Temporal dynamics: length of stay, hours since admission, rate of vital sign changes. (5) Demographic features: age, sex, admission type (emergency vs. planned). (6) Comorbidity indicators: ICD-10 codes rolled up into Charlson Comorbidity Index. All features are computed before the 30-day readmission window, ensuring no future information leaks into features.
structured_choices.model: Two models are trained and compared: (1) Primary: Bidirectional LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) RNN with 64 hidden units, trained on sequences of vital signs and lab results padded/truncated to 168 hours (7 days of hospitalization). The LSTM processes temporal dependencies and outputs a single prediction per patient. Architecture: embedding layer → bidirectional LSTM → dense layer (32 units, ReLU) → output layer (sigmoid). Hyperparameters tuned via grid search on validation set. (2) This RNN model is compared against the baseline.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the same engineered feature set as the RNN (vital sign statistics, medication counts, lab aggregates, demographics, comorbidity index). The logistic regression is not given raw time-series data — it receives the same derived features as the RNN, ensuring a fair comparison of model capacity rather than feature richness. Hyperparameters: L2 regularization strength (C) tuned via grid search on the same validation set using the same metric (see below). Justification: (1) Logistic regression is a strong, interpretable baseline appropriate for binary classification. (2) Both models receive identical feature inputs, isolating the contribution of the RNN's ability to model temporal dependencies. (3) Both models receive equal tuning effort — grid search over hyperparameter spaces on the validation set. (4) This comparison directly tests whether RNNs add value over traditional methods when the feature engineering is equivalent.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC). Justification: (1) The stakeholder goal is to identify patients at risk of readmission to allocate intervention resources. The ROC curve captures the trade-off between true positive rate (sensitivity: catching at-risk patients) and false positive rate (false alarms that waste resources). AUC-ROC is threshold-agnostic and summarizes performance across all decision thresholds. (2) Class imbalance is expected in readmission data; AUC-ROC is robust to imbalance (unlike accuracy). (3) The business context does not specify an exact cost ratio between false positives and false negatives, so a threshold-agnostic metric is appropriate for model selection.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC: Summarizes the precision-recall trade-off, particularly sensitive to the minority class (readmitted patients). Useful for imbalanced settings.
Sensitivity (True Positive Rate) at 90% specificity: Operationalizes a specific threshold — how many readmissions do we catch if we tolerate 10% false alarms? Ties to deployment decisions.
F1-score at the validation-set-optimized threshold: Balances precision and recall; useful if operational threshold is not pre-specified.
Calibration plot (predicted probability vs. observed frequency): Assesses whether predicted probabilities match true event rates. Critical for clinical decision-making.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 25–30) is used for all hyperparameter tuning and model selection. A grid search over hyperparameter spaces for both models (LSTM: hidden unit count, dropout, learning rate; logistic regression: regularization strength C) is performed by training each candidate configuration on the training set and evaluating on the validation set using AUC-ROC. The configuration with the highest validation AUC-ROC is locked in. The test set (months 31–36) is not touched during this process.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held completely locked until final evaluation. After both the RNN and logistic regression models are finalized (hyperparameters selected via validation set), predictions are generated on the test set exactly once. Primary and secondary metrics are computed on test set predictions. No further tuning, threshold adjustment, or model selection occurs after test set evaluation. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap or Wilson score intervals for proportions).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Hospital practice variability (discharge planning, follow-up care protocols) is a known confound that could affect both readmission rates and model performance. The temporal split naturally accounts for this: if hospital practices change over the 36 months, the model trained on months 1–24 is evaluated on a prospectively held-out period (months 31–36) that reflects current practices. This mimics real deployment. Additionally, stratification checks are performed: (1) Readmission rate is computed separately for each 6-month period within the training set to confirm no systematic drift. (2) Model performance is reported separately by admission type (emergency vs. planned), age group, and comorbidity burden to detect if the RNN advantage (or lack thereof) is specific to subpopulations. (3) Results are compared with site-wide quality metrics (LOS, mortality, discharge destination) in the evaluation period to ensure predictions are not artifacts of data quality changes. The experiment establishes that the RNN outperforms logistic regression on this dataset under these temporal conditions. It does not establish that RNNs are universally superior for readmission prediction across all hospitals or time periods — generalization would require external validation on data from other health systems.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNNs vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

We will use electronic health records spanning 36 months from a single hospital system, comprising approximately 100,000+ patient admission records. Each record includes time-series vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, temperature) recorded at 4-hour intervals during the inpatient stay; timestamped medication administrations; timestamped lab results; and discharge information. The outcome label is binary: readmission within 30 days of discharge, determined by cross-referencing discharge and subsequent admission timestamps. Patients with planned readmissions (e.g., scheduled procedures) are excluded from the analysis to ensure the label reflects unplanned clinical deterioration.

Known data quality issues to address: missing vital sign measurements (handled by per-patient mean imputation fit on training data only), lab result delays (handled by carrying forward the last valid result), and medication entry inconsistencies (standardized via medication terminology dictionaries before modeling). All data preparation is logged and versioned.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Because patient data is inherently temporal — vital sign trends and treatment trajectories build over the course of a hospitalization — we use a **temporal split preserving patient integrity**. Specifically:

- **Training set:** Months 1–24 (all admissions in the first 24 months)
- **Validation set:** Months 25–30 (admissions in months 7–12 of year 3)
- **Test set:** Months 31–36 (final 6 months of data)

All time-series data for a single patient is kept in one split; a patient's entire admission sequence is never split across train/validation/test. This prevents data leakage and ensures realistic evaluation: the model is trained on historical patient trajectories and evaluated on prospectively held-out patients it has never seen.

The temporal split also naturally accounts for hospital practice drift — protocols, staffing, and discharge procedures evolve over time. A model trained on year 1–2 data and tested on year 3 data mirrors real deployment where the model encounters slightly different distributions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are constructed at the patient-admission level and capture both static and dynamic characteristics:

**Vital sign features:** Mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum, and linear trend slope for each vital sign over the full hospitalization. These statistics summarize the trajectory and stability of physiology.

**Medication features:** Count of distinct medications, count of high-risk medication classes (opioids, sedatives, vasopressors), and duration on mechanical ventilation if applicable. These reflect clinical acuity and treatment intensity.

**Lab result features:** Abnormality flags (results outside reference ranges), trend direction (improving/stable/worsening), and count of labs ordered.

**Temporal dynamics:** Length of stay, hours elapsed since admission, rate of change in vital signs.

**Demographics and comorbidities:** Age, sex, admission type, and Charlson Comorbidity Index derived from ICD-10 codes.

**Preprocessing:** All transformations (imputation, scaling, aggregation) are fit on the training set only. Specifically, mean/median imputation statistics are computed from training data; standardization scalers are computed on training data; then applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents validation and test set characteristics from leaking into model training.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **primary model** is a bidirectional LSTM RNN designed to capture temporal dependencies in patient vital signs and lab trajectories. The architecture includes an embedding layer, a bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units (processing sequences of up to 168 hours / 7 days of hospitalization), a dense layer (32 units, ReLU activation), and a sigmoid output for binary classification. Hyperparameters (hidden unit count, dropout rate, learning rate, batch size) are tuned via grid search.

The **baseline** is logistic regression trained on the exact same derived feature set (vital sign statistics, medication counts, comorbidity index, demographics, etc.). Crucially, logistic regression does not receive raw time-series data — it receives the same aggregated features as the RNN. This isolates the contribution of the RNN's temporal modeling: both models have access to equivalent information, but only the RNN can exploit sequential dependencies. Logistic regression hyperparameters (L2 regularization strength) are tuned with equal rigor on the validation set.

This design fairly compares model architecture (RNN vs. linear) holding features constant, directly addressing the hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUC-ROC**. In the readmission prediction setting, stakeholders must decide which patients to target for intensive follow-up care. The ROC curve expresses the sensitivity (true positive rate: patients caught as at-risk) vs. 1 - specificity (false positive rate: unnecessary interventions). AUC-ROC summarizes performance across all possible decision thresholds and is robust to class imbalance. It aligns with the operational objective: maximize early identification of at-risk patients while controlling false alarms.

**Secondary metrics:**

- **Precision-Recall AUC:** Emphasizes performance on the minority class (readmitted patients), complementing AUC-ROC in imbalanced settings.
- **Sensitivity at 90% specificity:** Operationalizes a specific threshold: "If we tolerate 10% false alarms, how many readmissions do we catch?" Ties directly to resource allocation decisions.
- **F1-score at the validation-optimized threshold:** Balances precision and recall if the cost ratio is unknown.
- **Calibration plot:** Ensures predicted probabilities match observed frequencies — essential for clinical decision-making (e.g., a prediction of 30% should correspond to ~30% readmission rate).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set (months 25–30) only. Both models are trained on the training set with candidate hyperparameters, evaluated on the validation set using AUC-ROC, and the best configuration is selected. The test set is locked and not touched during this process.

### 7. Controlled Evaluation

After both models are finalized, predictions are generated on the test set exactly once. Metrics are computed and reported with 95% confidence intervals. We additionally report performance stratified by admission type (emergency vs. planned), age group, and comorbidity burden to detect if RNN advantage varies by patient subpopulation.

The experiment establishes whether RNNs outperform logistic regression for readmission prediction on this dataset and time period. Generalization to other hospitals or time horizons requires external validation.
